NÚMEROS: 10ª, 15ª, 100ª ligação

 

VISUAL: Representação visual de múltiplas chamadas bombardeando um prospect, destaque para saturação de cold calls

 

[10:02] And they always, a lot of them, the typical ones start with, oh, how are you doing today? Can I rub your feet? It's almost that kind of, that level of, oh, how's it going?

 

[10:14] And the first thing a business owner thinks when they hear that is, another one. So these are the techniques that are going to make you stand out from the crowd. So pay close attention.

 

[10:26] So like Sean just said, always just imagine, right? Before you even pick up the call, before you even dial, just know, just assume that you are the prospect 10th, 15th, 100th call of the day.

 

[10:40] How would you treat that call, right? You wouldn't treat that call as just a typical sales call. You put in a lot more effort. You treat it differently.

 

[10:50] And the cool part is that in IC, we learned about the Columbo technique, right? The pen and interrupt technique, right? Different opening techniques. So we're going to cover a few. It's going to be a recap for those of you that have been in IC for a while.

 

[11:06] But the key here is this. Use a technique that's most natural to you. Use a technique that works best for you. It's got to be muscle memory. It's got to be muscle memory. That's where you find the most effectiveness.

 

[11:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Controversial Truth: It Doesn't Matter What Opening Line You Use"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Statement provocativo destacado, ênfase em confiança e naturalidade vs. script perfeito

 

[11:22] And I'm going to give you, I'm going to prove a point to you, right? This might be very controversial to some, right? If you ask SDRs in the industry, US appointment setters in the industry, this is maybe controversial.

 

[11:35] But I personally believe that, to be honest, it doesn't really matter what opening line or technique you use, as long as you're confident, you're natural, and you've practiced it to its muscle memory.

 

[11:48] And hear me out. Hear me out, right? Before you shut me out, before you say, oh, Joel, that's not true. You can't just say whatever you want. Hear me out. Just hear me out real quick.

 

[12:00] When you do an FCC, remember what you learned in class. It's an interruption to a person's day. Am I right? You're interrupting them. You don't know whether they're in the shower. You don't know whether they're driving their car. You don't know whether they're having dinner.

 

[12:16] You don't know what you don't know. Let's put it that way. You don't know any of those factors you can't control, right?

 

[12:24] So here's my point, going back to the whole mindset thing. Just because I use a technique that worked now, or that did not work now, right, it doesn't mean you're going to get the same results for the next prospect because everyone is in a different place every time you call them.

 

[12:42] Different place, different emotions, different feelings. What if they were in the middle of an argument with their spouse when you called them? What if they were in the middle of work or driving when you called them?

 

[12:54] What if they were with their kids? What if they were on the way to the movies, right? There's so many factors that you can't control.

 

[13:02] What you can control, however, this is the key. Here's the key. What you can control is how convicted and how confident you feel with your abilities, with ultra high-ticket closer. Does it make sense, right? It goes back to the mindset.

 

[13:20] You can't control that. Look, put it this way. I could do the most perfect Columbo technique in the world. I could do the most perfect one. If the guy is having family time with his kids and he doesn't want to talk to anyone and he picks up, I'm going to get hung up on anyways.

 

[13:36] It doesn't matter how beautiful my Columbo technique is. Does it make sense, guys, right? The sexiest voice, the best accent, right? The perfect timing, perfect pace. He's going to say, look, you know, great, cool. I'm with my kids. It's not now. Boom, hang up.

 

[13:52] That's my point. It happens. It's life. It happens. It will happen to you. Let's put it that way. If it hasn't happened to you, it will happen to you. And that's the reality with FCC. That's the fact. That's the truth with FCC, okay?

 

[14:08] But what you can control is this. Look, hear me out. You know what? Prospects that are not interested right now, this is very key. Take this down.

 

[14:18] On your CRM or whatever thing you use to take your notes, your key points, make a task, make a reminder. Call back in 24 hours at 5 p.m. PST. Call back in 48 hours at 4 p.m. PST.

 

[14:32] That's what you can do. That's what you should be doing, right? That's the only thing you can control is that your follow-up, which we're going to get into later as well.

 

[14:42] But just keep in mind, I have to stress this because what we're going to see, right, in a couple days when you guys go and try this, you're going to be like, oh, but Sean and Joel taught us the Columbo technique, but I tried it. I got hung up on 10 times.

 

[14:58] Oh, I tried this, but, you know, I got rejected 50 times. You know, breaking news, right? Breaking news. There is no perfect script. There is no, yeah, someone said it, no one-size-fits-all, no magic pill.

 

[15:14] I say these words, boom, 100% close. There's no such thing. We got to wake up from that, and that's why the mindset comes so key.

 

[15:24] If I can add to that, Joel, I'm going to be honest with you, and the other part that I think I want to add to your mindset is if you're not prepared to get hung up on at least a number of times, then your mindset's not in the right place when you're starting out.

 

[15:40] And I'll tell you why. Because, like Joel says, it doesn't matter whether there is no 100% this is going to work with everything and everybody, but you have to make those mistakes.

 

[15:52] The only difference between the SDR that's successful and you when you're starting out is they failed more times than you have. You have to fail faster.

 

[16:02] So you have to be prepared for those hang-ups. You have to be prepared to make those mistakes, and you have to get in that rhythm. Because once you get in that rhythm, when the person hangs up, you'll just go, oh, I guess they weren't interested. Next.

 

[16:18] That's the mindset you've got to have. Thank you. Next. Right? As Sifu says, thank you. Next. Actually, no. Ariana Grande said that, right? Thank you. Next. But yes, next. Get my point. Anyways.

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Breakdown da técnica Columbo com elementos de confusão estratégica, tonalidade e pauses

 

[16:34] So let's go over a quick recap of the Columbo technique. All right, everyone. If you've been in IC for some time now, this is not new. You've probably heard this. We've taught this. We've went over it.

 

[16:48] This probably is the fifth or the sixth time you've actually seen this. So we're going to quickly go over this, right? So, Sean, would you be so kind to give me a call?

 

[16:58] Sure. Ring, ring. Hey, Joel speaking.

 

[17:02] Hey, Joel. I just got a note on my desk here from my assistant with your name and phone number. I'm guessing this is important. So how can I help?

 

[17:14] Oh, I don't really know what this is about, but okay.

 

[17:18] So, thanks, Sean. That's perfect. Now, I want you to notice, everyone, right? Not so much the words he said, but the way he said it. Sean, let's do it again one more time so everyone actually can focus on exactly what we're trying to get at. Call me again.

 

[17:34] Yeah. Ring, ring. Hey, Joel speaking.

 

[17:38] Hi, Joel. I just got a note on my desk here from my assistant with your name and phone number. I'm guessing that it's important. So how can I help?

 

[17:50] Everyone in the Discord chat, type in some key observations right now. What do you notice? Tonality, certainty, confusion, hesitation, certainty, pauses. Yes. Yes.

 

[18:04] Sean, do you have anything to add to what you were saying?

 

[18:08] I think I got a lot of it. You got a lot of it, and I don't know if anybody knows where the Columbo technique comes from, but Columbo used to be a private investigator series on TV, and he was the king of making it look like he didn't know what he was asking, but he knew exactly what he was asking.

 

[18:28] And he would be in that very, very almost that, you know, I'm not sure, but I should be asking this, but I'm not sure why. It's almost that sounding uncertain, but knowing in the back just how certain you are of the question that you're asking.

 

[18:44] But it is very much tonality. It's pauses. You're not, like if you come along and go, hi, I got this note on your desk with your name and number on it. It must be important. So what do you want? No, that's not. It's the slowness. It's the pauses. It's the tonality. That's what we're getting at.

 

[19:04] And it really, your prospect at the other end of the line will get that sense of, oh, this person's actually looking for help. Maybe I can help them.

 

[19:14] Yeah, and that's really so key, right? It's like what Sifu taught us as well. When we put people in a position where it feels like they can help you, human nature kicks in, right?

 

[19:26] They don't actively in their brain want to help you, but subconsciously they think, hey, you know what? This person sounds confused. Let me help them out subconsciously, right? And that's where it comes out. That's where the response comes out. Oh, I'm not too sure.

 

[19:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pattern Interrupt Technique: 'Hey, How Have You Been?'"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Técnica de interrupção de padrão destacada, diferenciação de tonalidade típica de vendedor

 

[19:44] And If you have time after class, do some research. Don't do it now. But Adam, I think Adam's kind of hitting the nail on the head there, right there.

 

[19:54] I mean, the hammer on the nail, right? Or the nail on the head. What am I saying? The hammer on the nail, the nail on the head there. It's really the truth of the yes. It's a pattern interrupt.

 

[20:08] Not only is it a pattern interrupt, right? Instead of saying yes, but when they say no, it primes them. It primes them differently to answer your other questions. It opens up different conversation, right?

 

[20:22] It doesn't prime them to then say no to everything else, right? But like Peter, exactly what you're saying. It diffuses the entire situation. You say no now, but then you can go yes, yes, yes, yes.

 

[20:34] So it's very fascinating when you look into the psychology of all this. And then the last one, hey, Adam, how have you been?

 

[20:42] Okay, now here is the part where, remember just now when we said don't act like a typical salesperson? Because I guarantee you someone's probably typing the question right now in Discord.

 

[20:54] But I thought you said not to say this. But I thought you said don't ask them how are they. But I thought you said don't ask them. Blah, blah, blah, right?

NÚMEROS: Baseline: <2% success rate vs. 6.6x increase com "How have you been?"

[21:38] Sales intelligence tool, just like Chorus.ai, they've analyzed thousands of calls. And read this, look at this response, okay?

 

[21:48] This phrase is a pattern interrupt that's unexpected and scrambles, I love that word, scrambles the prospect's brain in a good way.

 

[21:58] Sure, it may sound like we've met before, but it's not an overly unusual question to ask and will likely get a response that moves the conversation forward, right?

 

[22:10] So look at the two bars. The baseline is what the usual success rate is. Okay, look at it, it's super cool, right? Booked a meeting, that's a bam. You said a bam.

 

[22:22] The baseline is about like a two. Less than a two success rate using any other openings. But when they use how have you been, it's a 6.6 times increase in success rates.

 

[22:36] So don't tell me don't say how have you been. Don't tell me don't say how are you. Don't tell me what not to do. Rather, tell me how can I perfect, how can I make better, how can I have more conviction, more confidence in what I say. That's the key.

 

[22:54] The entire thing with step one is not to tell you really, oh, you know, when we say don't be a typical salesperson, it's the tonality, it's the behavior, it's all the tendencies of a typical salesperson, right? It's how you sound.

 

[23:08] We never said these words are forbidden, right? Because as you can see, you're not going to argue with data here. I can't argue with a 6.6 times increase in success.

 

[23:20] And by the way, this is what Sean, like Sean said, and I've tried this as well, it works. Hey, Sean, how have you been? It's the whole difference in tonality.

 

[23:32] If you come up to someone and go, oh, hey, Patrick, how you been? Versus, hi, Patrick, how have you been? Very much difference in how you say it.

 

[23:44] So if I called you up and I said, hey, Shang, how have you been? Right? I add that mysterious tone in there, right? Sort of like, you know how the inflection goes down?

 

[23:56] Hey, Shang, how have you been? Right? Sort of like the, what's that guy's name again? The freaking, what's the movie? The one, they kidnapped his daughter. God damn it. What's the movie? Come on, guys, help me out. Help me out here.

 

[24:12] Taken, yes, yes, Taken. How did I forget that movie name? It's kind of like Liam Neeson, right? Imagine that tonality. Imagine that, right? Sean, how have you been?

 

[24:24] Right? I mean, you don't over-exaggerate it, but just imagine if someone said that to you on a call, I'd be like, oh, crap. Who's this? Who are you? Tell me more. Wait, what is this?

 

[24:36] Okay, wait, who's this? I've been okay, but who's this? I'm okay. How are you? Right? Yeah, like Diego said, it could be the police. It could be an FBI investigator for who knows, right?

NÚMEROS: 2 (passo número dois)

[25:08] Okay, that was step one, right? So you picked up the phone. You picked up the phone. You've dialed the number, right? Remember, we're still on a journey here, visualization journey.

 

[25:20] You picked up the phone. You've dialed the number. Someone on the end of the line picks up, and you did your opening, right? Whichever one you picked. You picked whichever one you picked, right?

 

[25:32] Let's say I picked the Sean, how are you? Okay, done. Step one, boom. We're done with that. It's over. Now, step two, here we go.

 

[25:42] Assuming your opening is done well, done right, done effectively, prospect probably is in a good place in terms of, you know, physically has time to speak, right? Not driving, not out walking the dog, right?

 

[25:56] Maybe in his or her office, in his or her room. They have some time. They got a pen and paper, whatever it may be. They want to continue speaking. Here we go. Step two.

 

[26:08] You ready for this, guys? Yeah, I love that feel of that fish right there, that hook. That hook, here we come. Here it comes. This part is exciting.

 

[26:20] This part, I think everything else after this, I don't think we've ever talked about this or even taught this before. So a lot of this is all brand new stuff, okay?

 

[26:30] Now I'm going to hit them with a hook. So you came in. You did a pattern interrupt. You did the Columbo, right? It got them confused, got their brain scrambled, like what Gong said. Now you hit them with a hook. Sean, you want to take this?

 

[26:46] Yeah, because this is a place where I could go on a tangent for a long time. You know, you only have a maximum of 30 seconds, maybe at the outside a minute, to get someone's attention.

 

[26:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Attention Span Reality"

 

NÚMEROS: 30 segundos a 1 minuto máximo

 

VISUAL: Relógio marcando tempo curto, representação de atenção diminuindo rapidamente, comparação com era digital

 

[27:00] Everybody these days are looking for instant gratification. And with today's society, with where we get social media and all these things that we can get information quickly, people's attention spans are even that much shorter than they used to be.

 

[27:16] You know, you've got to have that key statement where, you know, you can say concisely why you're there, what you're talking to the prospect about. And then you want to immediately follow that up with a question after the hook.

 

[27:32] You know, this is such a key part of your call because this is going to make or break your FCC call. And this is what's going to set you aside from the average closer out there to a ultra high-ticket closer. This is going to be the differentiating part right here.

 

[27:50] And I have to just reemphasize, sorry, Sean, what you just said there is that this part is when we do a call review with our call audits of the different closers on our team. This is the part that gets people the most.

 

[28:06] They've practiced the opening, right? They've done Columbo over a thousand times on Discord. They've done the pattern interrupting over a hundred times, right? They've practiced in front of the mirror, all that. But this part messes people up so bad.

 

[28:22] Just imagine this, right? You spend days, right, hours preparing for your call. You did your 30 minutes of pre-call research. You've done all this good information, good research down, and you jump on a call, and within the next minute, you blow the entire thing.

 

[28:40] The entire call, the entire deal is off. It's done. It's nada, right? Probably close to no chance again in the future because you made such a bad first impression.

 

[28:52] They might not close with you. They probably close with someone else on your team. It happens. It could happen. Your first impression is so bad, they can't even trust you to guide them through the buyer's journey.

 

[29:06] This is what gets people the most, okay? Key, key. Pay very crucial attention. So Sean's going to demonstrate first. We should do what a bad hook is first and then what a good hook looks like. We should actually change that a bit.

 

[29:22] Yeah, let's skip over to the bad hook. Let's talk about that one first. Yeah, let's do this, Sean. Go ahead, call me. Do the opening and then this.

 

[29:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Bad Hook Example"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Exemplo negativo destacado em vermelho, demonstração de erros comuns

 

[29:34] Okay, so here, Joel, I'll call you. Hey, Joel, speak.

 

[29:40] Oh, hi, Joel. My name's Sean Lawrence. You don't know me. Have you got a few minutes to talk?

 

[29:48] Well, tell me what it's about really quick.

 

[29:52] I'm calling about Dragon 100. It's this advisory board that helps entrepreneurs scale their businesses. They have a lot...

 

[30:02] This part might surprise some of you. It might surprise some of you, it might not. But I will bet money it will surprise some of you.

 

[30:12] Because these are things that we've tested, that are proven within our department that got to work. And it's not only saved us, not only helped us close more deals, it helped us save so much more time, get better qualified BAMS, which leads to more sales. Okay.

 

[30:30] So Sean, are you ready? Yep. Here we go. All yours.

 

[30:36] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Key: Quickly Disqualify"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Conceito de filtro rápido, processo de qualificação destacado, ênfase em eficiência

 

[30:38] So the biggest point of an FCC call is that you want to quickly disqualify. Now I know that sounds backwards. So tell me in the chat, why would you want to quickly disqualify?

 

[30:52] Guys, you're going to get an aha moment here. You're going to get an aha moment here. Time management, that's definitely a big part of it.

 

[31:02] But really it goes back to, you know, Sifu said a number of times, you can only help those who swim towards you. In B2B it's no different. You have to find the people that you can truly provide value to.

 

[31:16] So again, and time management is the big part of it, is you're not going to spend a lot of time with the person that there's no value to what you provide for them. It's very much a, and we said it earlier, thank you, next.

 

[31:32] Back to that mindset. So in order to get to that point of being able to quickly disqualify, we have to ask the right questions to understand the prospect's business.

 

[31:44] So write this down. Ask questions to understand. Same as you would do with B2C, you do the same in B2B, but it's a different type of question because you're not talking about people's personal pain, you're talking about people's business pain.

 

[32:00] So you're asking questions at a surface level to qualify if there's even a potential fit for moving forward. So you guys ready for some questions that you could ask to find out, get those answers?

 

[32:14] I'm even going to, yeah, before I do that, Sean, really quick, I'm going to take a step deeper really quick, right? To just hit on that disqualifying part because I think that's so key. That's so critical. I can't even stress how critical this part is because yes, right?

 

[32:32] One part is great to save time, is great to talk to the people that want to be saved. But if you look at it even deeper, here's the reality, right? Well, I talked about in the beginning as well.

 

[32:44] Just because I'm not interested now, just because I say no right now, just because this is not the season in where my business needs the solution right now, doesn't mean I will never need your solution, your product or service in the future.

 

[33:00] This is the key part. It's just not right now. That's it. It's just not right now. Maybe not for a long time, maybe not for a couple months, not for years, not for weeks, not for days. It's just not right now.

 

[33:14] But we move on, we say, you know what? Thank you. Next. However, I think I saw someone put it in the chat just now already. I would add them back to the nurture list.

 

[33:26] I would add them back to my bucket that says not right now, maybe in the future. Not right now, send them content, send them marketing, hit them with more material.

 

[33:38] This is a key part, right? Because it's not just, oh, no, I'm not interested, throw the lead away, put the lead in a trash can, delete the lead.

 

[33:48] Yeah, Bob, then you're right. Not interested, it's a level of interest. I put this guy or gal back into my freaking nurture list, my bucket, and I go and I hit them with content, with good content, with good value, right?

 

[34:04] And this happens all the time. It will happen to you months later, years later, weeks later. The lead's going to come back and say, hey, you know what, Sean? I spoke to you six months ago. It wasn't a good fit for us that time. We had no budget. We were in a different direction.

 

[34:20] But now we're moving towards this industry or we're trying to expand. I think we need to talk more. Hey, it happens. I'm just saying it happens. It happens a lot, actually. It happens a lot.

 

[34:34] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Qualification Question #1: What's Working Well?"

 

NÚMEROS: Pergunta #1

[35:00] They're giving the best customer service they can to our actual clients because they're also severely affected. We can't produce enough machines for them. So, yeah, I guess customer service has been pretty good.

 

[35:14] So, Joel just gave me a whole bunch of information that I can drill down into deeper by asking that one question. I might be able to go, oh, well, you say you're not bringing in any revenue right now. Is that because of the current situation?

 

[35:30] And then he'd come back and say, well, Joel, how would you answer that?

[36:28] And there is very few people in this world that don't like talking about themselves or their business, even in bad times. Even in bad times. Yeah.

[36:58] But the easiest way to get someone talking is get them to talk about something that's important to them. Don't start with pain, get them talking. Exactly it, Chris. Just get them talking.

[38:04] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Qualification Question #2: Current Struggles"

 

NÚMEROS: Pergunta #2

 

VISUAL: Segunda pergunta destacada, abordagem para identificar dor de negócio

 

[38:06] All right, next question, Sean. So this is a little bit pain-centered, but this is definitely timely with where we are right now. Joel, what are the current struggles that you're facing right now?

 

[38:20] Yeah, like I said, we don't have a lot of orders coming in for our machinery. So production's slow. I had to lay off a few of my employees. I have a lot of struggle. Yeah, it's not fun. We don't know what to do really. The government's not really helpful.

 

[38:36] Now, this is also a question you've got to make sure you use the right tonality and the right frame to be able to deliver because you also have to, you need to make sure that you sound that you're genuinely interested in learning about your prospect and their business.

 

[38:54] Because if you ask this question of, so what kind of struggles are you facing right now? Are you having a lot of problems? No. You've got to show that genuine interest. Slow the question down. Ask it with that tonality that shows you're genuinely interested.

 

[39:12] And especially in these times right now, because a lot of business owners are all facing struggles, this is actually an easier question to ask than you think because everybody's looking for that, well, what can I do next?

 

[39:26] Yeah, in these times, they really resonate with it, right? Because right now, a lot of business owners are going through struggles. And this is actually becoming a much more common question in these times, during these moments.

 

[39:40] And yeah, Adam, I like what Adam just typed out there. You treat every call like the first. You give them the respect. And I think this is another key part where I really need you to write this down.

 

[39:54] Just because you're looking to quickly disqualify doesn't mean you just write them off immediately, right? Just because you think they said something that doesn't sound like, quote unquote, nice to you or right, you don't just write them off and just treat them like crap.

 

[40:10] And it's not like just, oh, you know what? They said to quickly disqualify. Well, I'm doing it. [40:26] Invest in getting his business going when he's more concerned about watering his flowers at home. Not a very good likelihood.

 

[40:34] Yeah. And as we said earlier, what do you do with people that say no right now, not interested right now, what do you do? Where do you put them?

 

[40:44] Yes. The nurture bucket, the marketing bucket, right? Either the remarketing bucket or your own content marketing bucket, whatever bucket you have with your team, your marketing division has it or your sales division has it, you put them back there, make a couple of reminders, follow-up tasks on your CRM and remember to call this guy back in three to four months.

 

[41:06] And the other thing that Diego put in the chat there that is key is you have to be detached. You can't be so hung up on getting that appointment. You have to truly be detached. If it doesn't work, disqualify, put them in the nurture bucket if it makes sense, move on.

[41:40] So when I said you guys are like a webinar, when I said we are acting like a trust funnel, like a webinar, like a filter, right? Let's consider some numbers here. I'll give you some real stats, okay?

 

[41:54] HTC, when we hit the HTC webinar, I've talked some numbers. You can have 100,000 viewers, 100,000 viewers. I would say about not even 20% will watch the entire webinar, okay?

 

[42:08] So 20% watch 100%. Let's say 50% of people, so 50,000 people watch half of the webinar. And let's say 70% watch 25% of the webinar. And the remaining 20, 30% of the bucket watch just 10% of the webinar.

 

[42:26] Some people register for the webinar, they don't even show up, right? 100,000 people. And you look at the number of people that actually book a call from the 100,000 people. I'll tell you the numbers right now. It's not a lot. It's like two to 5%.

 

[42:42] Even if you're lucky, you can get 5%, right? If you're lucky. Okay. Let's say 2%. So do some quick math, guys. What's 2% of 100K, Shang? Chris?

 

[42:56] All right. Chris is a pretty fast emerald. Shang, you're way too slow, Shang. Come on, you're like number 50. Okay, anyways. So 2,000 people, and that's over time, right? It's not gonna happen overnight, over time.

[43:26] People are not gonna show up to the call, right? They're not gonna show up. They're gonna reschedule. They're gonna cancel. Let's say, you know, good day, we close 20%. Okay. 2,000, what's 20% of 2,000, Chris?

 

[43:42] Oh, damn, Tiago, fast. Okay. Now, Chris, you're like Shang now. Shang's not even here now. I'm joking. I'm joking, guys. I'm joking. We need some humor. We need some humor in class, right?

 

[43:56] Okay, so Tiago, 400 people. Okay, great. Now, look at this, though. Look at the numbers. We went through 100,000 people to get to 400 sales.

NÚMEROS: 1,000 ligações = resultados garantidos

 

VISUAL: Desafio destacado, ênfase em volume e consistência de qualidade

 

[44:58] Call 1,000 people, I know how many people are gonna eventually buy, right? If I call 1,000 people a month, I know I'm gonna get X amount of sales every single month, no matter what.

 

[45:12] If you know that, you remember that, you calculate your daily income goal, your dig, right? Your daily, weekly goal, your daily, monthly goal. It helps you figure out how many calls you're gonna make a month to make X amount of money.

 

[45:28] And that's why I love the SDR role. That's why I like the appointment setting role because I can actually calculate that. And I actually believe in it because I trust the numbers.

 

[45:40] So I wanna give you a very deep perspective there because it's not like, this is why I don't wanna, we keep saying this, right? The breacher role, the striker role, the SDR role, it's not like the, what do you call it? The 10th child, you kick in the corner role.

 

[45:56] It's not like the hand-me-down, the rags, the shitty role. It's not that at all. It's like, that's such a disgraceful way of thinking, such a, I don't even know a word for it.

 

[46:10] That's such a embarrassing way to look at it because it's not like that at all. It's numbers. It's purely numbers. And then Pareto Principle, pretty much, yeah, it's numbers, right?

 

[46:24] So, okay, does that help, guys? Does that help you see a different perspective from the Emerald lens? So that's why I'm saying, I wanna give you some insights and inspiration, I think, I would say, inspiration to why this is so key.

 

[46:40] Don't get rejected just because you made 100 calls. Don't get rejected just because you made 500 calls. Do the 1,000 call first, you will. I feel confident. You do 1,000 calls, you're gonna get something. You get a sale, you're gonna get a couple bams, guaranteed, right?

 

[46:58] Get to that call, do it. Do a month, like, okay, do 30 days straight. Maybe not straight, but you know what I'm saying? Do a month, try to get 1,000 calls, depending on your industry, on your niche, right? On your product, on your offer.

 

[47:14] Just do 1,000 calls and then tell me if you don't get any sales. I'll give you guys a challenge right now. Do it and then tell me you don't get any results after 1,000 calls.

 

[47:26] But here's my challenge for you. You do it first and you show me that the first call, the 1,000th call, and the 500th call is all the same quality, right?

 

[47:38] Because I will guarantee you if you can do that, the same quality, it's impossible for you not to get results. I bet money on that. And challenge you, do it.

 

[47:50] And you tell me if you do it, if you don't get any results, I'll listen to your recordings. I say in class tonight, right? I say in class right now, if you do 1,000 calls, you don't get any results, I'll listen and I'll see what went wrong.

 

[48:06] But you will get something. You will get something out of it. [48:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Step 4: Close for the QC (Qualification Call)"

 

NÚMEROS: Passo 4, 24-48 horas (timing ideal)

 

VISUAL: Processo de fechamento para call de qualificação, urgência de agendamento

 

[48:30] So something that's very, like we were talking about earlier, on an FCC call, we don't try and close the sale on an FCC call. We want to get them to have, because an FCC call is typically a very, I tell everyone, an FCC call should be no more than four to five minutes because one, you've interrupted their day.

 

[48:50] You don't know what they could be doing. It could be like we just found out Joel was watering the plants. You don't know what they're doing at that time.

 

[49:00] So it's setting a time where you can actually get a qualification call set so that you can actually do that deeper dive, ask more questions to understand. And you want to make sure that as much as possible, you want to get that appointment within the next day or two days, within 24 to 48 hours.

 

[49:20] Now, type in the chat, why would you want to do that? Hotter, remind, refresh. It's very much a, you're catching them when you've already caught, you're already started getting their interest.

 

[49:34] The longer you leave it, and if you're Emeralds out there, if you want to know about it, just go read some of the statistics from Predictable Revenue and some of the other publications out there.

 

[49:46] The closer you can get it to the time you initially get their attention, the higher the percentage is that you'll actually turn it into a closing call with the AE. That makes sense?

 

[50:00] Very nice. Like I think of it this way, right? Like how many of you go fishing? How many people like fish, right? Like to fish, right? Go fishing. So when you hook a fish, right? Who leaves the fish out in the water for like more than like, you know, I mean, a couple hours maybe, depending...

 

[50:18] Guy still didn't show up. So it's better to just call and make it. Or if you do book it, I would still call the prospect even 24 or 48 hours in advance and just go, hey, we booked this appointment while you're away on holidays. Just wanted to make sure that's still a good time for you.

 

[50:36] And we got some bonus techniques that we will share in a bit that shows you how to increase your show up percentage rate. How many of you actually want to see that? Different things you can do to increase your show up rate, right? So yeah, fantastic. We're going to go into that as well.

 

[50:54] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Closing Script: Scheduling the QC"

 

NÚMEROS: 15-20 minutos (duração típica da call)

 

VISUAL: Script de fechamento para agendamento, exemplo de linguagem natural

 

[50:56] Cool. Sean, I think we kind of did this one already. We kind of, but it's just a little bit different twist, but we can do this one quickly. Let's go.

 

[51:06] So Joel, I know you said you have a pretty full schedule today and I want to respect your time. Could we schedule a call within the next day or two where we could explore things further?

 

[51:20] Yeah, I would like to explore things further. Are you thinking more like what, like 20 minutes, 30 minutes?

 

[51:26] Yeah, I need about 15 to 20 minutes of your time. Would say tomorrow or Friday morning work for you?

 

[51:34] Tomorrow actually works. What time zone are you in?

 

[51:38] I'm in mountain time. What time zone are you in?

 

[51:42] Pacific.

 

[51:44] Okay. So say eight o'clock your time tomorrow morning. Would that work?

 

[51:48] Yeah, that actually works actually right before my meetings. We can do that.

 

[51:52] Okay, perfect. So I've got you in here. You are familiar with Zoom, right, Joel?

 

[51:58] Yes. Oh yeah, we use Zoom all the time.

 

[52:02] Perfect. Okay, so I'll send you a Zoom link. We'll speak at 8 a.m. tomorrow morning and we'll just go over in detail how we can potentially help your business thrive in the current conditions. Sound good?

 

[52:16] Yeah.

 

[52:18] All right. I'll talk to you tomorrow, Sean. Thanks. Bye.

 

[52:22] Fantastic. Yeah, this is another one. This is a great one. I like this one because it gives context to why we need another QC because here's, I think this answers maybe Phil.

 

[52:34] I think Phil, your question was when you asked like if they want to just do the call right now, right? If you give them context as to why, you know, why you need a separate call like this, right?

 

[52:46] Hey, I want to cover things in detail. I need to ask you some more questions, right? See where I can help you. Do you have more time for an in-depth like discovery call, qualification call? That gives them the context, right? So Sean, you want to finish this as well?

 

[53:04] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Handling 'Let's Do It Now' Objection"

 

NÚMEROS: 5 minutos vs. 15-20 minutos necessários

 

VISUAL: Técnica para redirecionar urgência do prospect, manter controle do processo

 

[53:06] So Joel, there are a number of things I'd like to cover in more details so I can get to know your business better and see where our software could provide value to your team. Do you have time early next week for a more in-depth discovery call?

 

[53:22] I was hoping we could just do it right now. I got like five minutes left.

 

[53:28] I know that you got five minutes left, but like I say, I need to ask some in-depth questions and it typically takes about 15 to 20 minutes and I have another call I have to get on. So could we schedule something in the very near future, say tomorrow morning?

 

[53:44] Okay. Yeah, that's fine. That's fine. But like, what are you in? What time are you? I guess what time zone are you in?

 

[53:52] I'm in mountain time. You're in Pacific time, right? Is that correct?

 

[53:56] Yes.

 

[53:58] Okay. So 8 a.m. tomorrow morning, would that work for you?

 

[54:02] Yep, right before my meetings. That works.

 

[54:06] Perfect. Okay. I'll give you a call then. I'm going to shoot you, or actually I'll shoot you a Zoom link. You've used Zoom before, have you?

 

[54:14] Yes.

 

[54:16] Okay. So I'll shoot you a Zoom link and we'll talk at 8 a.m. tomorrow morning and go into the software in more detail. Sound good?

 

[54:24] Sounds good.

 

[54:26] All right. Thank you. Bye.

 

[54:28] Bye.

 

[54:30] And then the other cool thing about this, like when you give them context, you can also, something I like to do is like sometimes, and you guys put your hand up, you can't really put your hand on Zoom, right? In Discord, right?

 

[54:44] If you have experienced this before where the prospect just really, really wants to talk now, right? And they're like, I want to talk now. Like, I just want to do it now. I don't want to wait till tomorrow, right?

 

[54:56] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Cliffhanger Technique"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Estratégia de enviar informação parcial (teaser), criar antecipação para próxima call

 

[54:58] So I got a really cool thing that I like to do in order to kind of get them to diffuse that and get them on the next day, like to move them forward.

 

[55:08] Sometimes I like to say, hey, you know, I really appreciate that you want to do it right now. But truthfully, like I've got another call scheduled. Plus, I'm actually going to send you some PDF, some white paper or some content that you can actually refer to, right?

 

[55:24] So that actually saves time on our next call, right? So I'm going to send you the information. You can actually go over it. And that actually saves us some time. And it's a lot of questions that you would have, right, prior to our next call. We'll go deeper into the discovery question, right? We'll go deeper into questions.

 

[55:42] So sometimes if you diffuse it that way, then you will have less pressure, like I want to do it now, right? It's got to be done now. I want to talk to you right now.

 

[55:52] So diffuse it, read the requestiness, right? Explain the situation and send them something, right? And the trick is you don't send them everything you want to talk about, obviously.

 

[56:04] Because if you send them everything you want to talk about, then it's like, why do I need to jump on a call with you? So you send them like, you know, like a cliffhanger. You guys read books, right? Novels, fiction. It's like a cliffhanger, right?

 

[56:18] You send them some juicy information and you cut it. It's like, bang, talk to you tomorrow morning. Ha ha, right? It's kind of like that, right? It's kind of funny. It's like you give them the juicy part and then just cut off the rest. And it's like, oh, I'll talk to you tomorrow.

 

[56:34] Yeah, exactly. And then that also, see, this is another trick there. That also makes them more likely to jump on the call, by the way. I don't know whether you guys realize that, but that's actually, this is like a kill two birds with a stone kind of thing, right?

 

[56:48] You diffuse the situation. You make it juicy enough to make them want to jump back on the next call. Yeah, like Bobby, like a trailer. Exactly. Right. That's like one of my favorite things to do, right? Jump off the call, send them something juicy and then jump on the next call.

[72:12] Now you've got this blueprint that you can take, you can implement. It's super key for the next section that you also pay equal focus, equal attention, because I would say this part is really the bulk of where mistakes can happen.

 

[72:28] And it really is where the line between success and failure is, right? Because the FCC is only like a quick five, 10-minute call, right? Disqualify, set the next appointment, move forward.

 

[72:42] This next step, the QC part, right, which is right here. All right, if you mess this up, just imagine, right? The whole thing's off the table, right?

 

[73:04] And we've seen that a lot even within our teams. So this part, I would say, focus in. You want to role play this. You want to practice this in front of a mirror. You want to get this down, really, really nailed down, okay?

[74:14] And there's very different key components in both blueprints that you need to be very clear on. So if you have a Word document open, if you've got a notebook open, you want to make sure like you can split it by section, right?

[76:04] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "QC Step 1: Opening (Different from FCC)"

 

NÚMEROS: Passo 1

 

VISUAL: Abertura de QC vs. FCC, diferenças destacadas, evitar assunção de memória

 

[76:06] Okay. Same thing. Step one. You've got to open, right? You still got to open the conversation. You might be surprised just because they have it in their calendar doesn't really mean they actually remember or actually realize that you're going to call them. Doesn't really mean, right?

 

[76:24] It would be very foolish for us to assume that they're 100% expecting you. Does that make sense? Right? The biggest trap I've seen people fall in is when they call expecting the prospect to remember them and they stutter and they get lost of words because the guy forgot them.

 

[76:44] Okay, I forgot them. That's the number one trap I see people get because they think, oh, you know what? I booked a call from two days ago. This guy's going to remember me anyways. So let me call and just say, hey, you know, this is Joe. I spoke to you. I'm the guy that called you about, you know, Dragon 100, right? How are you doing?

 

[77:04] And the guy's like, like, who are you? Right? Oh, you know, refresh my memory. Like, what is this, right? Like, there's so many times that this will happen, especially if they're busy. They've been through a bunch of different events. They've got a bunch of call bookings, right?

 

[77:20] So the opening is super, super key. All right, Sean, are you ready?

 

[77:26] You betcha.

 

[77:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "QC Opening Techniques"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Técnicas de abertura únicas, pergunta inesperada, confirmação de tempo disponível

 

[77:30] So this, again, some of these points are still the same as you're talking about at an FCC call. You want to avoid being that typical salesperson. So you need to ask a unique opening question.

 

[77:44] One of our team members, one of his favorite lines to use is, what's the best part of your day so far? That's not something that someone's expecting you to ask.

 

[77:56] The other thing you want to do is you want to confirm that they still have time, that they have agreed for the duration of the call, you know, which will lead into the next part that we're going to talk about, you know, how to set up the call and move forward.

 

[78:12] But you need to just make sure, don't assume that just because you scheduled this call, something hasn't come up. You know, make sure, Joel, I just want to make sure we'd scheduled 20 minutes for this call. Do you still have that time available?

 

[78:28] Yeah, I have my calendar, so nothing's going to distract it. I should be good.

 

[78:34] Okay, perfect. And then I can continue on. So why not do it all the way from the top? So, like, we've done the FCC, we've booked the QC, and now you're calling me on the day of for the QC.

 

[78:48] Sure.

 

[78:50] Okay, so I'll give you a call. Ring, ring. Hello.

 

[78:56] Hi, Joel, it's Sean Lawrence calling. We had a call booked for today?

 

[79:02] Yes. You're the guy that was talking about Dragon 100, right?

 

[79:08] That's right, yeah. Just calling to follow up on our scheduled time. So tell me, what's the best part of your day so far?

[79:30] I was a bit worried, you know, they were not going to come back because of unemployment. Like, the unemployment pay is pretty high, and I'm kind of worried about that, but they're all willing to come back to work, and I think that's been really great.

 

[79:44] Well, that's great to hear. I'm glad they were able to come back. With getting started back up, Joel, we originally had 20 minutes set aside for this call. Do you still have that time available today?

 

[79:58] Yes, yes. You know, just hoping no one calls me back because, like I said, I've been calling employees. If they do, then I'll cut out for a bit because, you know, they're going to call my cell phone.

 

[80:12] Okay, well, I just want to make sure. If there's a better time, just let me know, but are you still okay?

 

[80:18] I think it's okay. I don't really – I probably wouldn't assume any incoming calls right now. I should be good.

 

[80:26] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Setting the Agenda (Frame Control)"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Estabelecimento de agenda, controle de frame, expectativas da call

 

[80:28] Okay, perfect. Well, on our call today, we're going to go more into depth to the struggles you're facing and see if our solution would be a good fit for your company.

 

[80:40] I'm going to need to ask some questions that might be a little bit challenging to answer, but I need you to be as open and honest with me as you can.

 

[80:50] Throughout the process, I will take note of any questions that you have so I can make sure they're addressed. If it looks like the solution we provide will be a good fit for your organization, then we can discuss what the next steps are. Does that work for you?

 

[81:08] Yeah, I think that's fair. I think that's fair.

 

[81:12] Yeah?

 

[81:14] Fantastic. And then a way to tweak it too round and they play by our rules, right? So exactly. It's exactly like what we learned in HTC.

 

[81:26] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "QC Step 3: Powerful Questions (DEEPER Level)"

 

NÚMEROS: Passo 3 - nível intermediário de profundidade

 

VISUAL: Diferenciação de perguntas: FCC (superficial) vs. QC (intermediário) vs. AE (profundo)

 

[81:28] Okay, now this part, the next part is extremely critical, okay? Because the step is the same. The step is the same, but the content is very different.

 

[81:42] So if you want to make it maybe more clear for you, you might want to say, you know, for the first one, right? Asking powerful questions FCC and at this point, you might want to say, asking powerful questions QC because it's different, okay? So Sean, take it away.

 

[82:00] So the biggest reason why our questions are different in a QC is because we're now going past the surface level. We're actually getting into those questions that ask, get deeper into their business and gives us the crucial information we need to know to make sure that this is going to be a good fit for the prospect.

 

[82:20] Again, you want to make sure that you've covered that WII FM, what's in it for me for the prospect. And again, this is not us as closers instilling our own values. This is really about what's important to your prospect.

 

[82:36] But the only way you can truly understand what's important to them is you have to ask those deeper questions. You know, it doesn't mean that you're asking the AE type deep questions. This is kind of a, think of it as a middle ground, right?

 

[82:52] You started with a surface level in your FCC call. Now you're getting into a little bit deeper, you know, just to make sure that you've got someone that's in the right frame of mind or in the right place to be, it'd be a good fit.

 

[83:08] But like we're saying, not deep level three pain, right? We're maybe still level one, even level two, but, you know, kind of that midpoint.

 

[83:18] And we're not digging for personal pain here. You're digging for business pains. This is a big difference between B2C and B2B. This is, you're helping them figure out what's going on in their business.

 

[83:32] Yes, that will affect what's going on in their personal life, but you're not there to solve their personal problems. You're there to help them solve their business problems. As a side effect might help them solve their personal problems.

 

[83:46] But the reason they're there is for their business, to make improvements in their business. So these questions that we're going to give you are going to be ones that will help you get into that deeper dive into what is important for their business. So you ready for these?

 

[84:04] Yeah, very well said, Sean. Sorry, I was just answering in, I think, sorry, I don't want to butcher your name, but Inz Page.

 

[84:14] So if anyone's asking what kind of pains are in B2B, remember to check out April 2020's IC class. Because in that class, Sifu and I, we covered the different kinds of B2B pain, right? You guys remember that, right? We've covered that in IC.

 

[84:32] So we talked about different types of level business pain. And in regards to Zachary, he's got a question for what is an AE type of question. So I think it's good, Sean. So why not talk us through what's the difference? What's an AE type question? And what's a QC type question?

 

[84:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "AE vs. SDR Questions: The Difference"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Comparação lado a lado: perguntas de SDR (qualificação) vs. AE (fechamento profundo)

 

[84:54] Okay, so let's talk about it in the context of Dragon 100, because that's something that was pretty top of my mind. And I know some people are kind of interested in how we qualify for Dragon 100.

 

[85:08] So for an example, one question we might ask a prospect in Dragon 100 as an SDR on the qualification call is we want to make sure that they have money that they're able to invest in their business to be able to improve their business and solve some of their business pains.

 

[85:28] But we don't talk as in the QC, we don't talk specifically about the price of the program. That's something that the AE would go over. So instead, we talk about their budget.

 

[85:42] So the questions we would ask are, well, Joel, do you have some money set aside that you could invest in your business to be able to help your business prosper or thrive in these current times?

 

[85:58] Yeah, I mean, when you say some money, do you mean like quite, like what is pretty significant?

 

[86:06] Well, it depends. For some businesses, it may be significant. But if you had to give me a number, do you have a number in mind of what you'd be able to invest?

 

[86:18] Right now, with everything going on, I mean, probably like not more than 10 grand, really.

 

[86:26] So if it was, now maybe you say 10 grand, is that what you have in your pocket right now? Or is it you need to split it up a little bit more? Maybe something that you could split over a monthly payment versus an upfront payment?

 

[86:42] That's what I'm comfortable with spending, for sure, because I have to keep a bunch of savings saved up in case things get worse, right? Shit hits the fan.

 

[86:54] Okay. So it's just what I'm comfortable with investing.

 

[86:58] For sure. So that's the kind of level, if you guys can see, is that I'm not actually telling him what the price is or whether he wants to buy. I just want to know whether he has money that he can invest.

 

[87:14] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "BANT Framework Explanation"

 

NÚMEROS: 4 elementos (Budget, Authority, Needs, Timeline)

 

VISUAL: Framework BANT detalhado, cada componente explicado para B2B

 

[87:16] Yeah. And like with Zachary, I'll give you another example, right? Like this is the difference between an AE type question versus an SDR QC question.

 

[87:26] So if I was going to ask, and I think James Adamson, he put in the chat there, BANT, which is great. So BANT stands for budget, B for budget, A for authority, right? N for needs, and then T for time, right? BANT, right? Authority also means decision maker.

 

[87:48] So if I was to ask a SDR, not necessarily will I go into, like if I was talking about the needs, right? Needs. So I might say, so I'm going to ask a question like this in the SDR call.

 

[88:02] Hey, Sean, what exactly do you feel is not quite right yet in your business?

 

[88:10] Well, you know, I'm still trying to figure out how to keep my employees engaged with, you know, having to work remotely. I mean, they're so used to working in the office right now.

 

[88:22] I'm just not sure how to make sure, you know, we do the Zoom thing and that, but I'm trying to figure out how to keep them engaged and keep them to keep pushing forward through this time.

 

[88:34] Got it. Okay. So this is a very key part. So Zachary, I hope you're paying attention, right? As an SDR, I may or probably not go deeper into that question.

 

[88:48] So when he tells me his issue is keeping employees engaged remotely, I'm going to write down, I'm going to take notes. Okay. Problem number one, having issues keeping employees engaged remotely. That might be my problem. Okay.

 

[89:04] Now as an AE, as an account executive, if I'm on a closing call, you better be sure I'm going deeper into that question. So I might ask Sean as an AE, Sean, so now that you're saying, you know, you're having issues keeping them engaged, how is it affecting your business exactly?

 

[89:26] Oh, you know, it's making it tough to keep new sales coming in the door because, you know, so many people have distractions of home and whatever their case may be. Like it's just not the same focus we have in an office environment.

 

[89:42] So you're saying it's actually affecting your sales revenue?

 

[89:46] Yeah, it is.

 

[89:48] Okay. And then see, so here as an AE, I push, right? And then I'm going to even push deeper. I can relate that. So, you know, talking about his sales revenue.

 

[90:00] Now, if you don't fix this problem, how is it going to affect your business's livelihood in the next 30 days?

 

[90:10] Okay. So, Sean, suppose this problem continues, how much of a buffer do you even have to be able to keep your business afloat if this keeps on going?

 

[90:24] Yeah, well, I mean, you know, we did pretty good last year. We put away about, you know, 50K, but it's, you know, with the way things are going and these guys not being focused, I mean, we're going to chew through that pretty quickly.

 

[90:40] So you're saying if you don't fix the whole engagement issue for your company from like working remotely, you're going to burn off your savings pretty quickly.

 

[90:52] Yeah.

 

[90:54] And I know you mentioned that, you know, the savings you've actually were planning to invest in, well, not invest, but rather build a bigger home, like you told me earlier, that you kind of promised your wife. So you're saying that if you spend this money, then you're not going to have that anymore.

 

[91:12] That's right.

 

[91:14] Yeah. So, Zachary, I'm giving you a kind of vague example there, but that's the difference, right? And as an AE, I would try to go as deep as I can when it makes sense, right?

 

[91:28] And then as an SDR, I'm just going to leave it at the, okay, you know, issue with engagement, remote, that's pretty much where I'm going.

 

[91:38] Okay, so I think Bob, I think Shang asked a great, I think Bob then asked a good question. I think Felix already answered it for you guys. Maybe Sean, you want to take it?

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Script para deflexão de preço, manter foco em fit vs. preço

 

[91:52] So it's kind of like if they really want to know about the price and, you know, like for example, our Dragon 100, right? We give the AEs the power to reveal it in their slide deck. How does the SDR handle that?

 

[92:08] So that's a great question. And I mean, what we tell our SDRs is it's pretty simple. It's, well, I'd like to get into that for you, but on this call, we're looking just to make sure that things are going to be a good fit.

 

[92:24] And if we do determine it's a good fit, we'd book a call with...

 

[92:30] Yeah, exactly, right? So I think with whatever products and services you guys have, think about all the different things. The best way I think about it is like, what's the value your product and service provide? What's the solution it does?

 

[92:46] If you can calculate it, if you can get it to a number point, so like this is a really emerald thing, right? Like if you can say each feature and benefit, and I know we don't want to talk about feature and benefit, but hear me out.

 

[93:00] You get each feature and benefit, you kind of calculate how much is it worth in terms of real numbers, right? Like dollars, whatever. You get the number. If you know that when you get on a call, you can easily kind of justify the value of it using numbers, right?

 

[93:18] Because you can always say to the business owner, I mean, you're losing X amount right now, right? How much would it be worth if you invested Y amount and fix your X problem? Then, you know, you could do that.

[94:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sifu's 5 Consultative Questions"

 

NÚMEROS: 5 perguntas fundamentais

 

VISUAL: Framework de 5 perguntas consultivas, aplicação em SDR vs. AE

 

[94:20] So here's a good question to ask. And again, you're getting the business owner to talk about their business. So Joel, what's working for you right now? And what is it that makes it work?

 

[94:34] I don't even know what's really working with. Everything's just recovering. I mean, I guess, you know, employees coming back. And I think what makes it work is that we have like, I treat them like family.

 

[94:48] And they probably see me as family too. And I think we work well as a team. So yeah, my employees, they're working well.

 

[94:58] That's good. So I mean, as much as, you know, some of that might seem a bit counterintuitive in our current situation, you're getting the person to think about something good, right? You're putting them in that positive mindset in the beginning.

 

[95:14] And like a lot of, that's why even a lot of improvement frameworks that are out there for businesses, you talk about what's working, what's not working, and how can you improve? So always start with a good first, right?

 

[95:28] Yes. So this way you get them to talk about what's going good. All right. Who remembers from the previous IC class, I believe it was two or three months ago, Sifu taught us the five questions to ask consultative closing. Who remembers that? Guys?

 

[95:48] Okay. Put down the five questions. Because I will show you some, we'll look at something that's really interesting. So when you find it, yeah, when you find it, like just paste it.

 

[96:00] Okay. So we'll go ahead for that until you guys find it. So Joel, tell me, what's your ideal vision for the company?

 

[96:10] Really right now, we, honestly, the next three to six months, I just want to make sure we get back to like, even what our revenue was, even 80% of what we were operating at the past quarter would be okay.

 

[96:26] So I'm just looking short term right now, because I need to make sure we don't go under.

 

[96:32] Again, you're getting them to talk about where they want to go. Perfect. Okay. Thank you so much, Dave.

 

[96:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The 5 Consultative Questions"

 

NÚMEROS: 5 perguntas

 

VISUAL: Lista das 5 perguntas: 1) What is working now? 2) What makes it work? 3) What's your ideal vision? 4) What's not quite right yet? 5) What tools/resources would make it right?

 

[96:42] What is working now? Okay, look at Dave's post, right? What is working now? What makes it work? What's your ideal vision? What's not quite right yet? What tools or resources?

 

[96:56] That's exactly it. These are the questions that you can ask in here as an SDR. If you look at these, what's not quite right yet? It's perfect. This is also a perfect question, great question for not just at the SDR level, but even the AE level. They can start here.

 

[97:16] But as an SDR, you definitely want to ask these questions because think about it. These are not too personal, but they're also going deeper than the surface, right? You see what I'm saying?

 

[97:30] You're not asking them about their wife and their kids and their husband and their dog, but you're asking them about their business. And so you're going deeper, but you're not going to the point where you're going to level three pain. So that's the whole purpose of it, right?

 

[97:46] As an AE, then I might go deeper into the why, right? The why, why, why, why. So which we'll talk about in a bit. Yep, what is not quite right yet? And then this one, Sean.

 

[98:00] So Joel, what tools or resources would make it right?

 

[98:06] Oh, I think we just need to know how to better use Zoom or I need to find a way to eliminate distractions from my employees. Like, you know, they got kids. I don't know what the best way to help them stay engaged, I guess. I don't even know what is the tool for that.

 

[98:24] So this is a part now where I could go and dig a little bit deeper. I'm not going to dig too deep as an SDR, but I might dig a little bit deeper because like, you know, the answer he gave me gave me some fairly high level stuff.

 

[98:40] So I might say, well, you say distractions. Tell me a little bit more about that, Joel.

 

[98:48] Yeah, well, what my employees are saying is that, you know, when they're working, like compared to working in the office where they're not distracted by family, at home, they got kids screaming, they got kids running around, you know, homeschooling the kids, doing homework, cooking meals for them, like all that kind of stuff. I think those are pretty big issues.

 

[99:12] Yeah, so they're definitely having a hard time focusing then with all the distractions of family and life around them then.

 

[99:20] Yeah. So, I mean, unless, you know, you guys have some kind of resource or tool that will help me, right now, that's the biggest pain in my, in my, in my, thorn in my side.

 

[99:34] Right. So you've also had a chance now not only to dig a little bit deeper, but also just, you know, read it, almost like read it back to them to make sure you've got an understanding of what their pain is, right?

 

[99:48] And these, all of these questions that we're asking too, these are notes that we're taking for when it goes up to the AE, right? Because the AE is going to look for the, what's some of the answers that you got and that's where they're going to start digging, asking even deeper questions, right?

 

[100:06] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The 7 Whys Technique"

 

NÚMEROS: 7 (perguntar "why" até 7 vezes)

 

VISUAL: Técnica dos 7 porquês, aplicação estratégica, encontrar propósito raiz

 

[100:08] Something that will help you. What I wanted to share earlier today, now, well, earlier in the evening, now that we're here is something I learned as well. It's like the whole asking why seven times. How many of you heard that?

 

[100:24] It's like a strategy, right? You ask why seven times and it's for communication, but I find that it really works well even for closing. So not in a rude way, like don't be like, why, why, why, why, right?

 

[100:38] It's kind of like, okay, so your employee is not having, you know, distractions. Why is that, right? And then they tell, oh, well, because the kid's at home. Huh, why do you think the kids at home are, you know, being that way, right?

 

[100:54] So like, also, you know, don't just ask why seven times and make it super awkward, right? You're not asking about the kids, it's kind of weird. So it's like, you need to figure out the level where you're going to use it.

 

[101:08] Yeah, so Adam's therapy, right? And then a lot of people ask about finding purpose. Like that's a very solid exercise, by the way. So like, if you haven't done that already, you just practice that with yourself, with your family, with your, you know, your spouse, with your friends.

 

[101:24] Ask yourself why, ask them why. That's going to make you a better closer. I guarantee that. If you can ask why at the right time, at the right moment, right? Still asking the right questions at the right time, you're going to ask very effective questions.

 

[101:42] Come on, William, that's funny. Yeah, that's true, right? So yeah, ask why, the right time, the right questions. But the deeper you can go with the right direction, it will make a lot of sense. It will help you move in the direction that you need to go.

 

[102:00] Yep. Okay, fantastic. We are getting close, everyone. We are getting, this is the fun part. This is the fun part.
