[102:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "QC Step 4: Close for AE Appointment (THE GOAL)"

 

NÚMEROS: Passo 4 - objetivo final do SDR

 

VISUAL: Importância crítica de qualificar antes de passar para AE, não desperdiçar tempo do AE

 

[102:44] Okay. This part is fun because this is, I guess, this entire part one of module three, I think lives for this moment, right? Like as an SDR, you will be, you know, you're close to the finish line when you can set the BAM for the AE, right?

 

[103:02] That's kind of the whole reason why you're doing all this, you're putting all this hard work is to get the BAM for the account executive. Otherwise, this AE jumps on and the AE messes everything up because here's the thing, the AE jumps on, the AE gets frustrated, the AE gets annoyed because the prospect's not qualified and then the AE might do something that turns the prospect off forever or the AE might not have the, so you just wasted, you just basically threw the ball to someone that's not ready to play.

 

[103:38] So like Sean saying something super key, don't set the bam, keep the bam, hold it off, do another QC, do multiple QCs, only set the bam when you're ready to give the ball to the AE because the AE is gonna freaking shoot the ball, right, or kick the ball. If the ball is not ready then you're not gonna score a goal.

 

[104:00] Because again, what's gonna put the money in your pocket? Is it gonna be setting the appointment or is it gonna be actually getting the boom?

 

[104:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "SDR Commission Structure"

 

NÚMEROS: $3,600-3,800 (Red Dragon), ~$5,200 (Black Dragon)

 

VISUAL: Estrutura de comissão baseada em performance, não em atividade

 

[104:12] Yeah, and by the way, you talk to different companies of different industries, I know some of them pay per lead or pay per appointment set, I get that, but here on our team, we don't freaking pay for activity. We pay for results. We pay for performance.

 

[104:30] You get a freaking big, okay, I don't mind revealing this, right, but Sean, how much does an SDR make from a Red Dragon sale? In US dollars.

 

[104:42] Yeah, it's over 3K. Like 3,600, 3,800?

 

[104:48] Yeah. And then SDR, how much do they make, like 4K? 4 something? I mean, sorry, Black Dragon.

 

[104:56] Yeah, it's even more. It's closer to, I think it's closer to 5, yeah.

 

[105:02] Yeah. So 5K for one sale, shit, give me 30 days, I make two of those, that's 10. Would you rather be paid for performance or would you rather be paid per hour or per appointment set?

 

[105:18] How about per appointment set, I pay you 100 bucks. That's it. You don't get any commission from the sale or you get a small one, or I pay you the huge commission for, exactly what the Sarge said, I pay you $5,200 for the sale.

 

[105:36] So yeah, on TDL, we don't pay for your BAMs, we pay for results. Exactly right.

 

[105:44] Yep. Okay. Awesome. We are getting there. We are getting there, everyone, we're getting to the team.

 

[105:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "QC Step 5: Make It Stick (Critical Handoff)"

 

NÚMEROS: Passo 5

 

VISUAL: Processo de handoff perfeito, edification do AE, comunicação com AE e prospect

 

[105:54] Make it stick. All right, let's recap the past four QC blueprint steps. Step one to four, what do we have so far? Because I want you to compare the differences, right? There are three that are the same, there are two that are different.

 

[106:12] Yep, opening agenda, powerful questions, close for the AE appointment. Yes, yes, yes, yes. And here, for the make it stick part, I'm going to share something else that's a big bonus that is not on the slide. So Sean, do you want to go ahead?

 

[106:30] So this is a part where you definitely want to make sure that you edify the account executive. So one thing you want to do, and if you don't have this information ahead of time, make sure you work and find out about your account executives.

 

[106:46] What's their background? Why are they going to be someone that this prospect wants to make sure they spend time with? Because if you edify them properly, your prospect is going to understand what value they will bring to the table and truly help qualify this prospect to make sure they're a good fit for the product or service that you're providing.

 

[107:10] You need to make sure that you communicate properly with your AE on the important details and next steps. So one of the things we have is we have a template, and this is why we ask all these questions.

 

[107:24] We get all this information from the prospect because we actually take all of this information, we put it into a template, and we pass that to the AE to make sure they understand all the stuff that you've, all the knowledge you've gotten about the prospect.

 

[107:42] But then you're also going to tell the prospect that, hey, we're sharing this information, as Aaron just said, a pre-call brief. We're sharing this information with the account executive so that they have some background on what we've already spoke about today, and it will help just make sure that the questions that the account executive is going to ask are going to be focused on what you're looking for for your business.

 

[108:10] And always allow your AE to easily access the call recording. On our team, it is mandatory that, unless a prospect outright says, you know, you cannot record my call, and they have that right because of privacy reasons, but otherwise, every call is recorded so that we can turn around and actually give that up to the AE because sometimes, you know, while it's great to give the notes and the pre-call brief, there's a lot of stuff like tonality and that or just stuff that gets lost in the translation of that pre-call brief, and the AE wants to have a second listen just because maybe they may pick up a couple extra things to help them deal with the prospect better, you know, a little bit more about, you know, their gem type or things that are other points that maybe you didn't note in your call.

 

[108:58] So like the Sarge says, we can't stress enough. It is super important to have both not only the pre-call details but that recording accessible.

 

[109:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Edification Script Example"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Script de edification do AE, colocar AE em pedestal, criar escassez de tempo

 

[109:12] And then Sean will demonstrate as well here with how he edifies the AE and makes it stick, which we'll go into in a bit. So we'll use the Sarge scene as he's here in the room.

 

[109:24] So Joel, I booked a call with one of our account executives. His name is Desmond Soon. Desmond's got years of business experience and firsthand knowledge of what challenges business owners are facing at this time.

 

[109:42] And because of that, it's putting his time in high demand. He's made some time available in his calendar to take your call. I know your time is valuable and his time is extremely hard to come by.

 

[109:58] So I just want to make sure that we don't waste anyone's time. If for any reason you're not able to make this call, can you let me know so we can reschedule?

 

[110:10] Yeah, that's fair. I don't want to waste an hour of anyone's time, much less mine, right?

 

[110:16] Yeah. See how I did that? Look at the, do you guys, can you guys point out the edification there in the, okay, put in Discord. How did Sean, or edify was the other way to think about it, you know, putting Desmond on a pedestal, right?

 

[110:34] How did Desmond, how did Sean edify Desmond? Put it in the Discord channel. What do you say? Yep. Years of business experience, first-hand knowledge, right? Challenges business owner, exactly, right?

 

[110:50] That key right there, the second sentence, second, third sentence there. And also his time is extremely hard to come by. Yep, you guys got it.

 

[111:02] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Make It Stick Bonus: Pre-Call FTC"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados no slide (técnica bônus)

 

VISUAL: Técnica secreta não no slide, call before the call, follow-up estratégico

 

[111:04] Now, yeah, we talked about edification quite a bit, I think over the past couple months in IC. So we kind of know what this is. So I'm going to share with you a couple tips and tricks that we used to do to ensure that it really sticks for the AE call.

 

[111:20] So what we talked about, this is similar, right? You send the email, you send the appointment, you send the pre-call text. Here is something that's not on the slide that I left out on purpose because I want you to actually write this down because it's not on the slide, you're not going to get this unless you listen to the recording.

 

[111:42] One thing that is super effective is I call it a pre-call FTC. Some people just call it the call before the call or the pre-call call, right? It's funny. But I call it the pre-call FTC.

 

[111:58] Before the prospect jumps on the call with Desmond, let's say Desmond's appointment with the prospect is on Thursday, right? Thursday afternoon. On Thursday morning or Wednesday evening, I FTC the prospect.

 

[112:16] Hey, Mr. Prospect, don't want to worry you, just wanted to quickly check in. You still good for the call with Desmond tomorrow, right? Do you get my email? I sent you some notes there. Desmond told me he reviewed your file. Is everything okay?

 

[112:34] I always recommend that. Whether it's the AE, probably not the AE that does that, but if you as an SDR, you do due diligence, like a proper handoff, right? You don't just pass the baton off when the guy is like 20 meters away. You just throw it to him.

 

[112:52] You run the baton right next to the guy, then you give the guy the baton, right? The next runner. You don't just throw the baton to the guy who's 20 meters away and they go, hopefully you catch it. Don't get us disqualified, right?

 

[113:08] So it doesn't mean I booked a bam for the AE and I go celebrate, right? Go drink in a bar or whatever. Oh, yeah, we did it. No, I still send all the information to Desmond, to the AE. I make sure Desmond has everything he needs. I make sure Desmond has asked me all the questions he has.

 

[113:28] And I send the prospect some emails. I even send a prospect a text. I will still call the prospect. I will still give the prospect an FCC. Hey, Mr. Prospect, just want to let you know, right? Just want to confirm you're talking to Desmond tomorrow.

 

[113:46] He actually told me to remind you. He talked to me earlier today and we just wanted to make sure you're good. Are you good? You okay? Everything's great? Fantastic. Well, I'll let Desmond know. If anything changes, don't hesitate to let me know, all right?

 

[114:04] That is the one crucial thing. If you do that, it's going to increase your shop rates a lot. Now, the same thing applies for B2C, by the...

 

[114:16] He could be the right person for you. Is that right?

 

[114:20] Yeah, that's right. Sounds good. And from what I hear from you so far, Sean, it seems like you're looking for some sort of the next steps as to what to do to expand globally. May I ask you, what kind of tools and resources would make it right for you?

 

[114:38] Yeah, well, that's where I'm kind of stuck. I know there's lots of... The technology side I have nailed down pretty good, and being able to talk to people remotely we have nailed down pretty good, but I just don't know, especially from marketing and lead generation, I don't know.

 

[114:58] I know there's ClickFunnels and all these other guys that you can build your own lead funnels and that kind of stuff, but because I've never dealt with them directly before, I've always gone through a second, third-party company, I don't know which one to pick.

 

[115:16] So maybe knowing which funnel building system would be a good one for us to start with, that might be a good one to go for.

 

[115:26] Okay, so knowing what kind of funnel and having some clarity as to where to go next would be the best approach for this. Is that what I'm hearing?

 

[115:36] Yeah, that'd be right.

 

[115:38] Okay, that sounds great. Okay, so that sounds good. I'm just making some notes here because I'm thinking about... It seems like we're a good fit here, Sean, and I want to ask you, have you been a part of any kind of, let's say, have you had a coach?

 

[115:56] I know you mentioned that you had worked with a digital marketing company before, but have you had a mentor? How coachable are you?

 

[116:06] Oh, well, that's an interesting question. I like to think I'm fairly coachable. I mean, I've worked with, like I said, I've trusted other groups to provide these services to me before.

 

[116:18] I don't think I'd be here right now if I wasn't willing to take some advice on the subject. So I think that's actually a really good question. Thank you. Do you have any specific examples you could share with me, Sean?

 

[116:34] Well, like I said, you know, the best example I have is that I also have a business coach that I work with, and he helps me on the strategy and where to take ourselves next.

 

[116:46] But, you know, even he's told me, he was the one that actually told me that I should go and look for someone who could give me advice in the lead generation and marketing side, because, you know, it would be a big benefit to my business to be able to have someone knowledgeable in that area to be able to mentor me and give me advice, rather than just, you know, trusting a third-party company that is a supposed expert.

 

[117:14] So I've taken his advice to actually reach out to you guys.

 

[117:20] I appreciate that, Sean. I can see that you are taking action based on the advice. So that's really good. Okay. Is there anyone else that would be involved in your decision-making process?

 

[117:34] No, I don't have any partners.

 

[117:38] Okay, great. And do you have any sort of budget set aside for this kind of investment?

 

[117:46] Well, I know that Dan Lok's time is expensive, but I've got about, I think I've got about $25,000 set aside right now.

 

[117:56] $25,000. Okay. Okay. $25,000. Okay. And how much do you think this could make you, suppose you were to take the right steps?

 

[118:10] Well, I don't know. I mean, I'd love to say, you know, 10 times return on my money is a minimum I'd love to get. But, you know, I mean, as much as possible, of course. I mean, who wouldn't want a big return on their money, right?

 

[118:26] Absolutely. I agree with you. Okay. Well, okay, Sean, what I can do for you next is I can pass you on to Michael Mason. He is our account executive, and he extremely, extremely, he has a lot of expertise in business.

 

[118:46] And what I can do for you here is to pass you on to him. And with him, he's going to dive in deeper about your current situation and how you could specifically help, or sorry, how you could specifically grow your business globally.

 

[119:04] Okay. Yeah, you would get the next steps there. Would it be helpful if I let you know, or sorry, would you like to move forward and book a call with him?

 

[119:16] Yeah, sure. I mean, sounds like he'll be able to answer any other questions I have or give me a little more detail, will he?

 

[119:24] Yes, I can also send you our white paper if that would be helpful.

 

[119:30] Sure, what's in the white paper? What would you like to see? I can send you multiple ones here.

 

[119:38] Oh, okay. Well, just maybe a little bit about how the process works with Dan Lok's consulting, for sure.

 

[119:46] Got it, how the process works. Okay. I'm looking at Michael's calendar right now. Does tomorrow at 2 p.m. work for you at your time?

 

[119:58] Yeah, it does.

 

[120:00] Okay, great. I'm going to book you in. And suppose anything were to happen, I know emergencies could just kind of jump up at you. Would you let me know so we could reschedule?

 

[120:14] Sure, yeah, I can let you know.

 

[120:16] Great, thank you, because Michael's schedule is really busy, and I'm sure he can add a lot of value to you. All right, and Sean, you have a great day. This is a pleasure talking to you.

 

[120:30] Okay, thanks very much.

 

[120:32] Thank you, thank you. Take care.

 

[120:36] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Role Play Feedback: Delia's QC Call"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Análise de performance, pontos fortes e áreas de melhoria

 

[120:38] All right, Delia, very nice. Nicely done, nicely done. I know we want to also have other people, so I'm going to give you some quick feedback, and then Sean can as well.

 

[120:50] So I like the most parts. I don't want to spend time complimenting you on what you did well, because you did a lot well. I want to focus on things you can improve, right, so you can implement.

 

[121:04] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "BANT Framework Application"

 

NÚMEROS: 4 elementos (Budget ✓, Authority ✓, Needs ✓, Timeline ✗)

 

VISUAL: Checklist BANT, destaque para Timeline como elemento crítico frequentemente esquecido

 

[121:06] So one thing is you did the BANT. So I should have made this clear. We should have made this clear in class, but one of the most simple frameworks in terms of qualifying a QC is to just go with the simple BANT model, right, budget, authority, needs, timeline.

 

[121:24] So you did the needs first, which is okay, right? It's good. And then you did the budget, and you did the authority, which is okay. The key thing, and this is what a lot of SDRs miss, is always the timeline.

 

[121:42] And let me tell you why this is so important, because, like, without knowing the timeline, and when I mean timeline, it's timeline from the end of the call, like the call with the AE, to quotation, to getting financing, to approve the payment, to getting the initial payments, to getting onboarded, to getting implementation of the tool, to getting delivery of whatever the solution is, that's the timeline.

 

[122:12] So people don't ask about timeline, so sometimes they're not sure what timeline means, and they don't really get, like, what timeline means, right? So, like, they don't ask it.

 

[122:24] But you need to ask for timeline because it involves a lot of factors, including some businesses, right? Their CFO or their finance team makes the final decisions on everything, or the CEO does, right? Now, you need to know that.

 

[122:42] And then also it ties into their implementation, right? How fast can you implement something like this? Because some businesses, they might be interested to do now, do it now, but depending on the solution, they might have, like, hey, you know what? Our implementation team is actually pretty busy right now, or our client onboarding team is pretty busy right now.

 

[123:04] They're stuck with new projects. It's something that we can only look at doing next month. And you need to let the AE know that because otherwise it'd be too premature to pursue the deal.

 

[123:18] So, for example, for Dan Lok Consulting, the timeline is not really huge. So just think about it this way, right, Delia? Timeline is like this. Okay, what needs to happen before Sean can jump on a call with Dan to get the consulting done and over with?

 

[123:38] Everything before that point is my timeline. So when I set the BANT for Michael Mason, that moment to the moment Sean jumps on a call with Dan, that's the timeline. What needs to happen between that period?

 

[123:56] So, one, first of all, obviously Sean needs to make the payment. Second of all, Sean needs to book a time with Dan, right? Then third of all, Sean actually needs to jump on a call, and then, you know, both parties need to show up. It's a pretty simple timeline, right, Delia? You get it?

 

[124:16] But you've got to ask Sean, hey, Sean, if this is something you're comfortable moving forward with, how soon can you get on a call with Dan? That's timeline.

 

[124:28] How fast or how, when would you like to get the consulting done with Dan, right? Because if Sean says, well, next month, it's like, wait, wait, wait, hold up. Let's say we're early May right now. Why next month, right?

 

[124:44] Oh, in next quarter. Wait, why next quarter, right? You've got to ask those questions to know. If Sean says tomorrow, you might want to manage expectations, right? Hey, you know, it's not really – first you've got to talk to Michael first, right?

 

[125:00] And Michael is going to walk you through the process, right? It's going to be payment, then scheduling, and then Dan's calendar is kind of booked right now, about a week or two weeks out. Is that okay for you if you had to wait two weeks? You have to manage expectations.

 

[125:18] So I know you don't know the Dan Lock Consulting product well, but that's typically how kind of a little bit more in-depth information for you to really capitalize on everything during COVID-19 so that way you can grow and scale your business. All right? Sounds good.

 

[125:38] All right. Thank you, Sean. Take it easy, okay?

 

[125:42] Okay. All right. Bye-bye.

 

[125:46] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Role Play Feedback: Phil's QC Call"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Análise crítica, falta de BANT completo, frustração potencial do AE

 

[125:48] All right, Phil. Finished. Good. Yes, sir. I kind of jumbled around a little bit.

 

[126:00] Yeah, I'm disappointed. You missed the entire bent. So when Desmond gets on the call, he's going to be pretty frustrated at you because he doesn't know whether this guy even has budget, right? Is he the only decision maker? We don't know.

 

[126:16] You got some of the needs, which is fine, but then you also missed the timeline. So like, yeah, these are things you definitely, for B2B, you want to go back and hammer it down in your head or in your notes.

 

[126:30] Like, I need to ask for budget, decision makers, needs, and timeline, right? Because you're going to run into, yeah, Desmond's going to, again, he's going to say probably not unqualified. He doesn't know. You don't know.

 

[126:46] And when Desmond asks you, did you qualify for this guy's bent? You're like, oh, well, shit, I forgot. Desmond will probably tell you, hey, I don't want to take the call. Go back on the call and talk to him first.

 

[127:00] So I'll be honest. I've never, this is the first time I've went over BANT. And just a question really quick, Joel, when I asked the question of how soon do you want to get this problem solved and how soon do you want to set up a time, is that not setting up a timeline correctly?

 

[127:18] More specific. How soon would you like to get on a call with Dan for consulting?

 

[127:26] Okay.

 

[127:28] You know what I mean? Like that's, because you, okay, the prospect, okay, here's the thing, right? And also here, talk about the concept of afters really quick.

 

[127:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Concept of 'Afters'"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Cliente não se importa com o call, se importa com resultados DEPOIS do call

 

[127:42] So afters is like, he, okay, Sean doesn't really care about the consulting call with Dan, right? What does Sean really care about? Capitalizing on the demand of his business right now.

 

[127:58] Yeah, Sean wants to actually see the money go in his bank, like the actual results of the call from Dan. So Sean doesn't give a crap. I mean, not doesn't give a crap, but Sean really doesn't care about the call with Dan at all. He cares about the effects, right? Of the call with Dan.

 

[128:18] So for timeline, sometimes, right? If you think about it for Danlock consulting, it might not just be, oh, you know what? You jump on a call with Dan, you're done. It's like, okay, you jumped on a call with Dan. How soon will you implement what Dan has consulted you with? And then you're going to implement that.

 

[128:38] Because that's true client success. If you really care about the prospect, you will walk them through all the way to them even getting the results past the sale. Does it make sense?

 

[128:52] So like I might ask, right, for timeline, it's like, oh yeah, cool. How soon realistically will you want to get on a call with Dan? And then, you know, he might say, well, in the next two weeks would be ideal.

 

[129:06] And I say, great. Now, once you finish the call with Dan, how soon do you think you can implement what Dan will consult you on? That to me is a complete timeline. Because it also shows the prospect that you care. Right? So yeah.

 

[129:26] Yeah, sorry. This is my very first B2B script. It's my first time. All right, Sean? I appreciate it.

 

[129:36] Yeah, no, if this was one of your first B2B ones, I mean, you're definitely headed along the right track. So kudos for that. Your tonality was probably one of the biggest things that I'd say that was really well.

 

[129:50] The end of it, you definitely, you were trying to follow the process, but you were starting to mumble and fumble and you lost your frame. And the biggest thing that I have advice for any closers is you cannot have all the answers.

 

[130:08] But if you maintain your frame and your tonality through the call, you cannot have all the answers until the prospect, you'll get back to them and you'll still get a result.

 

[130:22] But if you start losing that tonality and that frame and you fall apart at the end, the prospect might go, oh, wait, wait, wait, wait, something's not right here. You could throw your prospect off and then they could get cold feet or they could end up ghosting you. So that's, be careful of that. Make sure you hold that consistency all the way through.

 

[130:44] Yeah, I think it was just the lack of information of what exactly be consulting entails. So I think it's really down my confidence a little bit, but I understand. Thank you.

 

[130:56] And you're going to get into situations where you're closing for products that you don't know all the answers. Don't let that get you down. Don't let that throw you off.

 

[131:08] Make sure, because there's nothing, anybody I've worked with in B2B, and then maybe you got to have another QC call, might be, you know, I don't have that answer for you right at this moment, but can I get that answer for you and we can schedule another call and then we can continue further. Would that make sense?

 

[131:28] Right, so you're not, don't feel that you got to fumble your way through. Just make sure that, you know, if you don't know, honestly, don't be afraid to say, well, you know what, I got to find that answer for you.

 

[131:42] Okay, sounds good. I appreciate it.

 

[131:46] All right, thank you so much, Sean and Phil. Awesome, awesome. Great, great night. Yeah, I think we all learned a lot. A lot of implementation was done as well.

 

[132:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q&A: Forgot BANT - What to Do?"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Solução para erro de SDR, fazer outro QC antes do AE call

 

[132:02] I think Inc's got a question, one last question in terms of, hey, if I forget to do the BANT, right, can I just call back at pre-call or can I do another QC?

 

[132:14] I would recommend you do another QC before the actual call because the AE is not going to be happy that you didn't even qualify the person. You might as well just, you know, I mean, if you can, do the other QC prior to the actual call of the AE, but you don't want to let your AE walk into, like, it's like you're kind of setting up your AE for failure, if that makes sense, right?

 

[132:38] You also want to care for your AE. Don't give AE like a broken ball or like a deflated ball that has like holes in the ball, right? So yeah, you want to make sure you clean that up to make it good for your AE. Make the ball good, solid, right? You can kick the ball, it works fine, then that's cool.

 

[132:58] So whatever it takes to fix that for sure. Because again, the AE is going to ask you for the BANT. They're definitely going to ask you whether you check for that, right?

 

[133:10] I would say, don't make the mistake, just put it in your brain somewhere, write down BANT, that's the solid thing. And just like, you know, the old saying, happy wife, happy life, happy AE, good for me.

 

[133:26] I like that, happy AE, good for me. I love it.

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO (CONTINUAÇÃO)

 

NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS (ADICIONAIS):

$3,600-3,800 comissão SDR por Red Dragon sale

~$5,200 comissão SDR por Black Dragon sale

~20 pessoas no B2B Foundation Class original (CIT exclusivo)

2 horas de duração do B2B Foundation Class

24 horas para release do conteúdo exclusivo

June 9th, 6 PM PST - Module 3 Part 2

 

ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS (ADICIONAIS):

Pre-Call FTC (Call Before the Call): Ligar para prospect antes do AE call para confirmar, aumentar show-up rate

Perfect Handoff Process: SDR roda o "bastão" até AE, não joga de longe

AE Edification Script: Years of experience + high demand + scarcity de tempo

Pre-Call Brief Template: Todas informações do QC organizadas para AE

Call Recording Mandatory: Exceto se prospect recusar, sempre gravar para AE revisar

Multiple QC Strategy: Fazer quantos QCs necessários antes de passar para AE

7 Whys Technique: Perguntar "why" até 7 vezes para encontrar raiz do problema

Afters Concept: Cliente não se importa com call, se importa com resultados DEPOIS

Frame Maintenance: Manter tonalidade e frame mesmo sem todas as respostas

 

ESTRUTURA (ADICIONAL):

QC Blueprint Completo:

Opening (diferente de FCC - confirmar memória + tempo)

Agenda Setting (frame control, expectativas)

Powerful Questions (nível intermediário, BANT)

Close for AE Appointment (qualificar completamente)

Make It Stick (edify AE, pre-call brief, recording, pre-call FTC)

 

BANT Framework Detalhado:

Budget: Quanto podem investir? (não revelar preço)

Authority: Quem toma decisão final?

Needs: Quais problemas de negócio? (não pessoal)

Timeline: Quanto tempo até implementação completa?

 

Sifu's 5 Questions (aplicação SDR):

What is working now?

What makes it work?

What's your ideal vision?

What's not quite right yet?

What tools/resources would make it right?

 

SDR vs. AE Question Depth:

SDR: Surface to intermediate (level 1-2)

AE: Deep dive (level 3 pain, personal connection)

 

DADOS FINANCEIROS (ADICIONAIS):

Comissão baseada 100% em performance (boom), não em atividade (BAMs)

2 Black Dragon sales/mês = $10,400

Estrutura de pagamento incentiva qualidade vs. quantidade

 

ROLE PLAYS ANALISADOS:

John: Bom opening (Columbo), hook confuso (misturou produtos), faltou make it stick

Bogdan: Excelente performance, "real sign of UHTC", pattern interrupt perfeito

Delia: Boa execução geral, faltou Timeline no BANT (erro comum)

Phil: Primeira tentativa B2B, faltou BANT completo, perdeu frame no final

 

DURAÇÃO TOTAL: ~2 horas 16 minutos FORMATO: Aula ao vivo Inner Circle - Module 3 Part 1 com role plays extensivos PRÓXIMA SESSÃO: June 9th, 6 PM PST - Module 3 Part 2 BONUS CONTENT: B2B Foundation Class (2 horas) será liberado em 24h
